coredata.dc:title: Amine functionalized carbon quantum dots from paper precursors for selective binding and fluorescent labelling applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Lisa John, Varsha
Joy, Francis
Jose Kollannoor, Amy
Joseph, Kuruvilla
Nair, Yamuna
T. P., Vinod
coredata.dc:description: We report a novel synthesis route for preparing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) of customized surface functionality from readily available precursors. The synthetic strategy is based on the chemical modification of paper precursors prior to preparing CQDs from them. The pre-synthesis modification of paper precursors with (3-Aminopropyl) triethoxy silane (APTES) enabled us to synthesize CQDs with amine functional groups on the surface. The silane coupling via condensation between the ethoxy group of APTES and the cellulose hydroxyl group on the paper resulted in the tethering of amine groups on the paper substrates, which are retained as surface-bound species during the synthesis of CQDs from the modified paper. Amine functionalization on the surface of CQDs helped us use them in applications such as DNA binding. We analyzed the interaction of CQDs with calf thymus DNA (CT-DNA), and the results imply their propensity as an efficient biological probe. The synthetic strategy presented here can also be extended to other functional groups.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Modified carbon quantum dots
Photoluminescence
DNA binding
DNA cleavage
amine-modified CQDs

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of APTES modified CQDs (PP-CQD-NH2 and FP-CQD-NH2)
Anilide synthesis from PP- CQD-NH2 and FP- CQD-NH2
Synthesis, purification and structural characterization of CQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the synthesis of amine functionalized CQDs.
The HR-TEM images at different magnifications with the corresponding size distribution histograms of CQDs: (a-d) PP-CQD (e-h) PP-CQD-NH2 (i-l), FP-CQD (m-p) FP-CQD-NH2.
Raman spectra of CQDs: (a) PP-CQD (b) FP-CQD (c) PP-CQD-NH2 (d) FP-CQD-NH2.
IR spectra of CQDs: (a) PP-CQD (b) PP-CQD –NH2 (c) FP-CQD (d) FP-CQD-NH2.
XPS survey spectra of CQDs: (a) PP-CQD, (b) PP-CQD-NH2, (c) FP-CQD, (d) FP-CQD-NH2.
Deconvoluted XPS spectra of C (1 s) and O (1 s) in CQDs: (a,b) FP-CQD, (c,d) PP-CQD.
Deconvoluted XPS spectra of C(1 s), O(1 s), and N(1 s) in CQDs: (a-c) PP-CQD-NH2, (d-f) FP-CQD-NH2.

UV–visible absorption, PL excitation and emission spectra of CQDs: (a, b) PP-CQD, (c,d) FP-CQD, (e,f) PP-CQD-NH2, (g,h) FP-CQD-NH2, the wavelength of excitation (λex) corresponding to the emission spectrum being 240 nm.
Excitation wavelength-dependent PL emission spectra of CQDs: (a) PP-CQD (b) FP-CQD (c) PP-CQD-NH2 (d) FP-CQD-NH2 ranging from excitation wavelength 220 to 400 nm with 20 nm increments.
Tauc plots depicting bandgap and the comparison of the calculated wavelength with λmax.
The plot of fluorescence decay of the synthesised CQDs, the wavelength of excitation (λex) corresponding to the emission spectrum being 280 nm.
Electronic absorption spectra of PP-CQD-NH2 in the absence and presence of increasing amounts of CT-DNA. (b) Linear plot for the determination of intrinsic binding constant (Kb) of PP-CQD-NH2.
Electronic absorption spectra of FP-CQD-NH2 in the absence and presence of increasing amounts of CT-DNA. (b) Linear plot for the determination of intrinsic binding constant (Kb) of FP-CQD-NH2.
Fluorescence spectrum of CT-DNA upon addition of various concentrations of (a) PP-CQD-NH2 (b) FP-CQD-NH2.
(a) Fluorescence Quenching of PP-CQD-NH2-DNA system induced by various concentrations of EB. (b) Determination of Stern Volmer quenching constant of PP-CQD-NH2.
CD spectrum of CT-DNA upon addition of PP-CQD-NH2 and FP-CQD-NH2.
Gel electrophoresis pattern of test compounds. Lane 1: DNA Marker; Lane 2: CT-DNA; Lane 3: CT-DNA + DMSO; Lane 4: FP-CQD-NH2; Lane 5: PP-CQD-NH2.
The plot of % viability and concentration of test compounds.
Details of Absorption and Emission studies.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: where, Kb = Binding constant determined from fluorescent titration

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Stern-Volmer quenching constants.
Quenching constants of EB-DNA (CQD) systems.

head.ce:title: Amine functionalized carbon quantum dots from paper precursors for selective binding and fluorescent labelling applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Hosur Road, Bangalore, Karnataka 560029, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Hosur Road, Bangalore, Karnataka 560029, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Indian Institute of Space Science and Technology, Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala 695547, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Indian Institute of Space Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Indian Institute of Space Science and Technology, Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala 695547, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a novel synthesis route for preparing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) of customized surface functionality from readily available precursors. The synthetic strategy is based on the chemical modification of paper precursors prior to preparing CQDs from them. The pre-synthesis modification of paper precursors with (3-Aminopropyl) triethoxy silane (APTES) enabled us to synthesize CQDs with amine functional groups on the surface. The silane coupling via condensation between the ethoxy group of APTES and the cellulose hydroxyl group on the paper resulted in the tethering of amine groups on the paper substrates, which are retained as surface-bound species during the synthesis of CQDs from the modified paper. Amine functionalization on the surface of CQDs helped us use them in applications such as DNA binding. We analyzed the interaction of CQDs with calf thymus DNA (CT-DNA), and the results imply their propensity as an efficient biological probe. The synthetic strategy presented here can also be extended to other functional groups.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a unique class of zero-dimensional (0D) nanomaterials that were discovered serendipitously in 2004 during the fractionation of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNT). They have been proved to be of remarkable applicability in the optoelectronic, biological, and catalytic arena [1]. CQDs generally consist of a graphitic core, and Hydrogen atoms or functional groups saturate the shell layers. The quantum confinement effect (QCE) facilitates the formation of discrete quantized energy levels within CQDs that results in the density of states (DoS) and thereby a bandgap [2]. Unflickering photoluminescence (PL) observed in CQDs has been primarily attributed to QCE [3]. The CQDs are preferred over semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs) in many of the practical applications of fluorescent systems due to their biocompatibility, low toxicity, fluorescence stability, resistance to photobleaching, excitation wavelength-dependent emissions, chemical inertness, and facile synthetic routes [4]. These features make CQDs a versatile system having potential applications in areas such as optronics, sensing (chemical/biological) [5], biomedicine, etc. [6,7].

The synthetic methods for CQDs are classified broadly as top-down and bottom-up methods, based on the change in dimensions from the precursors to the CQD products. The former refers to cutting down significant precursors to obtain CQD. In contrast, the latter aggregates organic molecules under thermal/hydrothermal/solvothermal conditions, followed by carbonization [8]. The synthesis of CQDs from natural and readily available material precursors is a progressing trend due to their easy availability, comparatively cheaper and greener routes for the synthesis, albeit low quantum yield (QY), and purity [9].

Enhancing photophysical properties by modifying CQDs via surface passivation, heteroatom doping [10,11], etc., is another area of active research in this field [12]. Surface passivation of CQDs via polymers (PEG 1500N, PEI, etc.), amino acids, functional groups (–OH, –NH2, etc.), ionic liquids ([BMIM]BF4, [BMIM][NTf2], etc.), etc. are efficient strategies for alleviating the contaminants and aggregation, that are capable of tuning the properties of the system [13,14]. These species bind to CQDs via covalent and non-covalent (π-π stacking, electrostatic, complexation, etc.) interactions. The enhancement of QY is an additional possibility of surface functionalization. The sonochemical methods, pyrolysis, microwave irradiation, etc., are the commonly adopted routes for surface functionalization apart from the hydrothermal/solvothermal methods.

Herein, our work demonstrates a novel approach for introducing amine (–NH2) modification on CQDs synthesized from paper precursors. The possibility of synthesizing CQDs with desired functionalities from readily accessible precursors following a facile route is demonstrated here. As a proof-of-concept demonstration, we used printing paper and filter paper as precursors, and treatment with a solution of (3-Aminopropyl) triethoxy silane (APTES) functionalized these precursors. CQDs were consequently synthesized from these modified paper precursors following simple steps such as burning and sonication. This method differs from previous reports presenting the synthesis of surface-functionalized CQDs as the precursors are modified before the CQD synthesis. A covalent bond induced due to modification of paper precursor with APTES ensured the availability of accessible amine functionalities on the surface of CQDs prepared from them, facilitating specific applications such as DNA binding.

The printing paper (75 GSM) to synthesize the CQDs, labelled as PP, was purchased from a local store. The filter paper to synthesize the CQD, labelled as FP, was procured from Lab Filter Paper. (3-Aminopropyl) triethoxy silane (APTES), Quinine Sulphate (QS), Sodium Hydroxide, and Benzoyl Chloride were purchased from Sigma Aldrich and Ethanol from Prince Chemicals, Bangalore, India. The Vasa Scientific Co, Bangalore supplied the Dialysis Membrane-70 (Flat width-28.46 mm, Diameter-17.5 mm, molecular weight cut-off of 12000 Da to 14000 Da) and syringe filters (0.22 μm).

The major constituent of papers is cellulose fibres that are compressed into sheets during paper making process[15,16]. The paper pieces with an area of 3x3 cm2 were burned to ashes and dispersed in 10 ml distilled water. After two minutes of ultrasonication, a 12-minute probe sonication (Labmatrix, Anamatrix Instrument Technologies Pvt.Ltd.) was carried out with a pulse rate of 5:1 and 70% amplitude at 28 ° C. Syringe filtration followed by the dialysis of the filtrate for 24 hrs yielded PP-CQD and FP-CQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of APTES modified CQDs (PP-CQD-NH2 and FP-CQD-NH2)
ce:section.ce:para: The modification strategy for paper precursors was as per previous reports [17]. The papers with 3x3 cm2 dimensions were immersed in the solution of APTES (100 mg APTES in an 80:20 mixture of ethanol: water) for 2 h. The solvent was evaporated at 40 ° C, and the papers were thermally heated for 2 h at 110 ° C. The so obtained papers were washed with ethanol and were allowed to dry at room temperature. The paper precursor is thus modified with amine functionalities, and following the procedure in section 2.2 led to the synthesis of amine-modified CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anilide synthesis from PP- CQD-NH2 and FP- CQD-NH2
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of benzanilide was according to the principles of the Schautten-Baumann reaction. To the 5 ml solution of CQDs taken in a conical flask (with stopper), 45 ml 10% NaOH and 7 ml Benzoyl Chloride solutions were added. The mixture was shaken vigorously for 10–15 mins until the crude benzoyl derivative separated. The synthesis of benzanilide was confirmed by IR spectroscopy.

The quantum yield (QY) of CQDs was calculated with the following equation [18], where 'x' and 'st' corresponds to the sample and reference standard, QS, respectively. The QY of QS in 0.1 M H2SO4 is determined to be 54%. When Q stands for quantum yield, A, I, and η refer to the absorbance, fluorescence intensity at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, and refractive index of the solvent, respectively.
Q x
=
Q st
.
A st
A x
.
I x
I st
.
I 2
x
I 2
st

The Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) analysis was carried out using Perkin Elmer Spectrum 100 series spectrometer in a frequency range of 4000–500 cm−1 with a spectral resolution of 4 cm−1. The X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) analysis had been performed on a PHI Model 5802. The Raman spectra were recorded from a Raman spectrometer (INVIA, England) with a 514 nm Argon ion laser from 100 to 4000 cm−1. The X-ray Diffractometer analysis was performed with Bruker D 8 Advance with Cu-Kα radiation whose wavelength λ is 1.5406 Å, operated at 40 kV and 20 mA in the range of 10° to 90°. The High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) images were captured with JEOL JEM-2100, LaB6 operated at 200 kV. The surface topography of CQDs was studied with a Park System XE 100 Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) in non-contact mode with a silicon probe having < 10 nm tip. The absorbance was recorded on the Shimadzu UV-1500 spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) were recorded using Shimadzu RF 5301 spectrofluorometer. The PL decay (lifetime) measurements were recorded on a JOBIN-VYON M/S Fluorocube-lifetime system. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) data were recorded on Zetasizer (ZS90). The roughness of paper substrates was obtained from an optical profilometer (Zeta 20).

The absorption spectral titrations were carried out to determine the intrinsic binding constants of CQDs with calf thymus DNA (CT-DNA) by the incremental additions of DNA to a fixed concentration of the test compound. DNA was dissolved in tris buffer (NaCl 50 mM, Tris HCl 5 mM) by maintaining the pH in the range of 7.2–7.4 to prepare the CT-DNA stock solution. The protein-free state of DNA was confirmed by calculating the absorbance ratio at 260 nm and 280 nm, which was greater than 1.8. By employing the molar extinction coefficient 66000 M−1 at A260 nm, the concentration of the nucleic acid solution was determined. Aliquots of DNA solution were successfully added to a fixed concentration of the probe, and the corresponding absorption spectra were recorded. For the probe to interact with DNA, each addition was followed by 2 min of equilibration. The intrinsic binding constant was calculated using the equation
(1)
[ D N A ]
( ε
a
-
ε b
)
=
[ D N A ]
( ε
b
-
ε f
)
+
1
K b
(
ε b
-
ε f
)
where,
D N A
= concentration of DNA

ε A
= Apparent molar extinction coefficient of the compound

ε B
= molar extinction coefficient of the compound in bounded form

ε F
= molar extinction coefficient of the free compound
ii.
Fluorescence spectroscopic studies

The fluorescent titrations were carried out by the incremental addition of the probe to a fixed concentration of CT-DNA. The excitation and emission slit widths were set to 5 nm. For each data set, the response time (0.04 s), excitation voltage (700 V), and scan rate (1500 nm min−1) were kept constant. The fluorescent quenching experiments were carried out using ethidium bromide (classical intercalator), and the corresponding quenching constants were found using the equation
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 +
k q
τ 0
Q
= 1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]
where F0 and F are fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of quencher (test compound), respectively; KSV is linear Stern-Volmer quenching constant; [Q] is the concentration of quencher and τo is the average fluorescence lifetime of the biomolecule (τo = 10-8 s) in the absence of the quencher.
iii.
DNA Fragmentation Studies

The test compounds were dissolved in DMSO and were added separately to the CT DNA sample. The sample mixtures were incubated at 37 °C for 1 h. The gel plate was prepared and loaded into the electrophoresis chamber flooded with Tris-acetate-EDT buffer (TAE). The DNA sample with bromophenol blue was carefully loaded into the wells, along with a standard DNA marker and a constant 100 V of electricity was passed till the dye front reached the end of the gel. The gel plate was removed carefully and was stained with EB solution (10 μg/ml) for 10–15 min. EtBr was destained from the gel plate, and the bands were observed under UV transilluminator.
iv.
Cytotoxicity studies

MTT Assay is a colorimetric assay that measures the reduction of yellow 3-(4,5-dimethythiazol- 2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) by mitochondrial succinate dehydrogenase. The assay depends both on the number of cells present and, on the assumption, that dead cells or their products do not reduce tetrazolium. The MTT enters the cells and passes into the mitochondria where it is reduced to insoluble, dark purple coloured formazan crystals. The cells are then solubilized with DMSO and then released. Solubilized formazan reagent is measured spectrophotometrically at 570 nm.

The MTT Assay evaluated cell viability with six probe concentrations in triplicates with three independent experiments. Cells were trypsinized, and the tryphan blue assay was performed to know viable cells in the cell suspension. Cells were counted by haemocytometer and seeded at a density of 5.0 × 103 cells / well in 100 μl media in 96 well plate culture medium and incubated overnight at 37 °C. After incubation, the old media was taken off, and 100 μl of fresh media was added with different concentrations of the probe in represented wells in 96 plates. After 48 hrs, the solution was discarded and fresh medic with MTT solution (0.5 mg / mL−1) was added to each well, and the plates were incubated at 37 °C for 3 hrs. At the end of incubation time, precipitates are formed due to the reduction of the MTT salt to chromophore formazan crystals by the cells with metabolically active mitochondria. A microplate reader was used to measure the optical density of solubilized crystals in DMSO at 570 nm. The percentage of growth inhibition was calculated using the following formula.
(3)
% I n h i b i t i o n =
100 × ( C o n t r o l - S a m p l e )
C o n t r o l
The IC50 value was determined using a linear regression equation, i.e. y = mx + c. Here, y = 50, m and c values were derived from the viability graph.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, purification and structural characterization of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 1 depicts various steps involved in the synthesis of amine functionalized CQDs. We used dialysis for the purification of CQDs. We attempted an additional purification step in the form of column chromatography. The samples purified through dialysis were eluted through a silica column with 1:1 vol ratio of acetonitrile: Millipore-water used as the eluent. We observed a decrease in concentration of the CQDs and diminished quantum yields after the chromatography process. ( Figure S1
, Table S1, Table S2, Supplementary material). The hydrophilic, hydrophobic, π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding properties possessed by CQDs can result in their attachment to silica, interrupting the elution process [19]. The decrease in the concentration of CQDs in our samples also can be attributed to these attachments. Also, while recording the fluorescence of CQDs with a 1:1 vol ratio of acetonitrile: Millipore-water as the solvent, a considerable decrease in PL intensity was observed ( Figure S2 , Supplementary material), which we assume maybe one of the reasons (in addition to the decrease in concentration) for the decrease in quantum yield after applying chromatography using this solvent. Other common solvent combinations (e.g. water/methanol, acetonitrile/methanol) were not giving proper elution of CQDs. Based on these obseravtaions, we restricted the purification of CQDs to the filtration and dialysis processes.

The treatment of printing paper and filter paper with a solution of APTES yields amine functionalities on their surface. Treatment of the paper precursors with a solution of APTES brings about silane coupling via condensation reaction of the hydroxyl groups of cellulose (on paper) and ethoxy silane moieties (Fig. 1) [20]. CQDs were synthesized from these modified paper precursors, and the presence of amine moieties on the CQDs (PP-CQD-NH2, FP-CQD-NH2) were detected using several analytical techniques. We conducted a control experiment to examine the possibility of APTES modification on CQDs prepared from non-modified paper precursors. The PP-CQD and FP-CQD samples were treated with APTES solution for 5 h, resulting in the quenching of fluorescence, probably due to the aggregation of particles. The particle size analysis from DLS data supports this conclusion ( Figure S3, S4
, Supplementary material), signifying the importance of the pre-synthesis modification of paper precursors.

The HR-TEM images and the corresponding size distribution histograms confirm the synthesized CQDs' quasi-spherical morphology and narrow size distribution (Fig. 2
). The CQDs display discrete lattice fringes of 0.21 nm and 0.34 nm separations, corresponding to the (100), (002) planes of carbon-based materials. The XRD plot (
Figure S5
, Supplementary material ) also corroborates this observation [21]. Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) analysis indicated a uniform height distribution ( Figure S6, S7
, Supplementary material). The average sizes of CQDs range between 3 and 5.5 nm, except in the case of FP (12 nm). The aggregation tendency of FP-CQD is evident from AFM and TEM.

The existence of different functional groups confirmed via the above-discussed characterizations should affect the surface charges of these CQDs. The zeta potential analysis of all four samples was conducted, and the effect of pH on the surface charges was analyzed. The shift of potentials to positive values in PP-CQD-NH2 and FP-CQD-NH2 reiterates amine modification (Table S4, Supplementary material). A flip in the zeta potentials observed while altering the pH correlates with the role of pH and functional groups in tuning PL of CQDs [26]. The alleviation of the zeta potential of CQDs with sonication accounts for the perturbation to the SiOC bond on their surface (Table S5, Supplementary material).

If free amine groups existed on PP-CQD-NH2 and FP-CQD-NH2, they should form the corresponding derivatives, which led to our attempt to synthesise the corresponding anilide from PP-CQD-NH2 and FP-CQD-NH2. We could synthesise anilide derivatives from PP-CQD-NH2 and FP-CQD-NH2. A control experiment conducted with PP-CQD and FP-CQD, which couldn't furnish an anilide in a similar reaction condition, confirmed the feasibility of secondary modification on CQDs only for the amine-modified samples. These conclusions were obtained from IR analysis ( Figure S8
, Supplementary material).

The UV–visible absorption spectra of the CQDs displays the contributions of intrinsic π-π* (230–270 nm) and n-π* (300–330 nm) transitions emerging from the graphitic cores and the surface defects, respectively, with a tail extending upto visible region (Fig. 8
). The emission spectra showcase a bathochromic shift for the 240 nm λex because of the surplus functionalities on the CQD surfaces[27] (Fig. 9 d,h). The QY of these systems is predicted to be low due to energy loss and unwanted light absorption [28]. The higher QY of PP-CQD-NH2 and FP-CQD-NH2 than PP-CQD and FP-CQD is due to the surface states induced by the additional amine modification on the surface [29] (Table S6, Supplementary material). This enhancement of QY implies the probability of tuning bandgap and thereby PL by the amine functionalization.

The excitation wavelength-dependent red-shifted emissions of the CQDs explain the reason for the enhanced QY in PP-CQD-NH2 and FP-CQD-NH2 furthermore (Fig. 9 ). The surface states generated due to the functional groups instigate different emission levels between the highest occupied molecular orbital and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (HOMO-LUMO) energy levels. These energy states alter emissive recombination's transition probabilities, resulting in a redshift with low energy excitation wavelengths [30]. The emissions recorded in Fig. 10 consist of double peaks corresponding to high and low energy aggregates formed within the systems under study [24]. The fluorescence emissions of PP-CQD-NH2 and FP-CQD-NH2 are dependent on pH and ionic strength ( Figure S9
A, B, Supplementary material). The PL intensity increased under both acidic and basic conditions. The broadening of peaks is attributed to the hydrogen bonding interactions between the functional groups [31]. The PL properties were studied by varying the concentration of KCl in a dispersion of CQDs from 0 to 18 μM in neutral conditions. A gradual increase of intensity was observed with increase in ionic strength. The ionic strength of the salt is known to cause aggregation of CQDs, resulting in the enhancement of PL [32].

Absorption spectroscopy is the most common and efficient instrumental technique to study the interaction of various probes with DNA. The binding efficiency and change in binding properties can be analysed by observing the absorption spectra of the DNA-bound probe. In general, different physical and chemical agents bind with DNA via two binding modes: (i) intercalation due to π stacking or dipole-dipole interactions and (ii) surface binding (grove binding) due to electrostatic or hydrogen bonding interactions. The absorption spectra for PP-CQD-NH2 and FP-CQD-NH2 were recorded with incremental additions of CT-DNA, and the respective binding constants were calculated to study the occurrence of these events in our systems (Figs. 12, 13
). In the case of the CQDs without amine functionalization (PP-CQD and FP-CQD), there was no regular trend in absorption and emission titration curves observed, attributed to their poor DNA binding capacity.

The probes (PP-CQD-NH2 and FP-CQD-NH2) showed hyperchromism upon incremental addition of CT-DNA. PP-CQD-NH2 exhibited a slight blue shift with hyperchromism, while FP-CQD-NH2 exhibited redshift with hyperchromism. In general, hyperchromism takes place due to the destruction of the secondary structure of DNA upon interaction with the probe.[37] The interaction forces that hold the double-helical structure of DNA are ruptured, separating it into two single strands. [37] The disruption of the double-helical structure by the probe is mainly due to the intercalative mode of binding, which is assumed to be occurring through the surface binding via the hydrogen bonding interactions between the amino functionalities of the probe and the phosphate groups of the DNA backbone. The DNA strands are known to wrap around the CQDs, thereby inserting the protruding amino groups of the probe into the double-stranded DNA resulting in partial intercalation [38]. The binding constant for PP-CQD-NH2 (Kb = 74.015 (mg/μL)−1) determined from absorption spectral studies is found to be higher than FP-CQD-NH2 (Kb = 62.23 (mg/μL) −1) (Table 1 ). The hyperchromic effects of PP-CQD-NH2 and FP-CQD-NH2 can be attributed to the combined effects of an intercalative and electrostatic mode (hydrogen bonding) of binding between the probe and the DNA.

In the study of genomics and proteomics, emission enhancement upon binding the fluorescent probes with biomacromolecules like DNA and proteins is essential. The addition of CQDs into DNA solution forms a CQD-DNA adduct, thereby exhibiting emission enhancement upon incremental addition of CQDs. The emission enhancement of the probe-CQD complex can be attributed to the extent to which the fluorescent probe gets into the hydrophobic environment of DNA. A decrease in emission intensity of CQDs upon binding with DNA indicates the interaction of CQDs with DNA ( Figure S10
, Supplementary material).

Ethidium bromide (EB), a cationic dye, is an intercalator that efficiently binds to DNA [40]. EB can be utilised for competitive displacement assay to determine the binding affinity of unknown probes with DNA. It replaces the probe from the probe-DNA complex, resulting in a more stable EB-DNA complex. A significant decrease in the emission intensity was observed upon adding EB to the probe-DNA complex (Fig. 15 ). This highlights that the emission enhancement is due to the formation of the CQD-DNA complex and not the increase in the concentration of unbound CQDs. The displacement of CQDs by EB from the DNA helix indicates an intercalative mode of binding where the amino groups effectively insert into the DNA helical structure while it wraps around the CQDs. Additionally, the protruding amino groups tend to form hydrogen bonds with the phosphate oxygen present in the DNA backbone culminating in the surface binding of the probe with DNA [41].

The competitive binding assay carried out for PP-CQD-NH2 and FP-CQD-NH2 led to the determination of the quenching constant (Ksv), signifying the probe's binding efficiency with DNA. A high value for the quenching constant is a clear indication of an excellent binding efficiency of the probe (Table 2 ). The differential binding efficiencies of the synthesised CQDs might be due to the varying number of amino groups and/or the difference in the wrapping of DNA around the probe. In conclusion, CQDs adopt a mixed-mode of binding, which includes partial intercalation via insertion of amino functionalities into the double-helical structure and the formation of hydrogen bonds with the phosphate groups of the DNA backbone.

Experimental and theoretical investigations were carried out to prove that the conformational stability of DNA decisively depends on the ionic strength [21]. The effect of monovalent ionic concentration on probe-DNA binding was effectuated by studying the DNA binding properties in the presence of an increasing concentration of NaCl (0, 0.15, 0.30 M). An increase in ionic strength is known to decrease the binding of the probe with DNA as the positively charged ions screen the negatively charged phosphate groups present in DNA, and the chain segments come into intimate contact. At lower salt concentrations, the repulsive interactions between the negatively charged phosphate groups along the DNA backbone predominates, which keeps the DNA segments far apart, thereby helping the probe to intercalate between the DNA base pairs efficiently. At a very high concentration of salt, the DNA helical structure is tightly interwound, highly bent and very compact, which prevents the intercalation of the probe into the DNA duplex, thereby reducing the binding affinity [42].

Determining binding strength by varying the ionic concentration is also an effective tool to study the binding mode. The reduced binding affinities with increased ionic concentration indicate the possible intercalative way of binding. With an increase in ionic concentration, the quenching constants of both EB-DNA (PP-CQD-NH2) and EB-DNA (FP-CQD-NH2) decreased drastically (Table 3
,
Figure S11 (Supplementary material)). The high ionic concentration precludes the binding of EB to CT-DNA, which in turn proves an intercalative mode of binding of the probe with CT-DNA.

The binding mode of the probes (PP-CQD-NH2 and FP-CQD-NH2) can be easily determined by comparing the CD spectra of the bound and unbound DNA. The DNA duplex displays a canonical B- form conformation, characterised by a positive band at 275 nm in the CD spectrum, attributed to the base stacking interactions in DNA [43] (Fig. 16 ). Furthermore, the right-handed helicity of DNA can be characterised by the negative band seen at 210 nm in the CD spectrum. In general, small molecules binding to DNA duplex via electrostatic interactions or groove binding do not significantly affect the base stacking and the helicity of the B-form of DNA. On the other hand, molecules that intercalate the DNA duplex tends to perturb the base pair stacking and the helicity, thereby causing disturbances in the positive and negative signals in the CD spectrum. It turns out that upon adding PP-CQD-NH2 and FP-CQD-NH2 to DNA, a significant change in the signal is observed, clearly indicating a strong interaction of the probe with DNA. The disturbances observed in the spectra might be due to the intercalative interaction of the test compounds with DNA that evidently perturbs the base pair stacking and the helicity.

Developing novel therapeutic agents for infectious diseases include designing and fabricating small molecules targeting nucleic acids, ultimately leading to its damage. The graphitic core and the protruding amino groups are key factors that make the synthesised CQDs a potent intercalator that efficiently binds to DNA which is clear from the preliminary analysis carried out (emission and absorption titrations). The planar aromatic rings present in the graphitic core helps to efficiently insert and stack between the base pairs of the double-helical DNA. The amino groups enhance the binding affinity by forming hydrogen bonds with the base pairs and phosphate groups.

Hence to determine the cleavage efficiency of PP-CQD-NH2 and FP-CQD-NH2, a DNA fragmentation assay with CT-DNA utilizing agarose gel electrophoresis was carried out. The cleavage efficiency was analysed by comparing the bands formed with the probe and the control in the gel plate. After binding to DNA, the probe induces several conformational changes and deformations due to bending, over winding, under winding and micro loop formation, leading to changes in the molecular weight of DNA, which gets reflected in the migration rates as seen in the gel plate (Fig. 17 ). The criterion for evaluating the difference in DNA cleavage depends on the intensity and bandwidths compared to the control. The formation of smear in lanes 3 and 4 without any sharp bands, as seen in the control, indicates the cleavage of CT-DNA by the probes. Moderate cleavage efficiency is exhibited by both PP-CQD-NH2 and FP-CQD-NH2, which is characterised by the migration of the smear. The smear formation indicates the denaturation of the hydrogen bonds between the base pairs of CT-DNA at multiple sites. Moreover, the cleavage repeatedly occurring at numerous sites leads to the formation of low molecular weight fragments which might have migrated away from the gel during the onset of electrophoresis [44,45].

In the ontogenesis of chemotherapeutic agents, the evaluation of cell cytotoxicity is one of the most significant indicators. Certain physical and chemical agents interact with proliferating cells and inhibit their growth via various mechanisms, an essential attribute in the generation of anti-proliferating agents in active immunotherapy. The cytotoxicity of PP-CQD-NH2 and FP-CQD-NH2 was evaluated with MCF-7 cells using in vitro MTT assay. It was found that both PP-CQD-NH2 and FP-CQD-NH2 are cytotoxic, which can be confirmed from the cell viability and IC50 values (Fig. 18 ). The synthesised CQDs led to the loss of cell viability by disrupting the cell metabolism. The reduced cell viability with increasing concentrations of CQDs can be accounted for the damage caused to DNA which might have signalled the cells for apoptosis. The IC50 values obtained for PP-CQD-NH2 (73.24 ± 0.843) and FP-CQD-NH2 (75.37 ± 0.519) are comparable with FP-CQD-NH2 exhibiting slightly higher value compared to PP-CQD-NH2. The concentration-dependent drop in cell viability affirms that the cell viability decreased drastically with increased CQDs concentration.

We presented a new experimental strategy for preparing CQDs with customized surface functionality. Pre-synthesis modification of the precursor introduced the desired functional groups on the CQD surfaces. Commercially available paper substrates were first modified with APTES, and the CQDs prepared from these modified precursors carries amine functional groups on the surface. The free amine groups present on the surface of CQDs enabled the usage of CQDs as an efficient biological probe. The DNA binding capability of these CQDs (PP-CQD-NH2, FP-CQD-NH2) was studied and attributed to the combined effects of an intercalative and electrostatic mode (hydrogen bonding) of binding. The synthetic strategy presented here can be extended to preparing CQDs carrying other functional groups on their surfaces.

Varsha Lisa John: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Francis Joy: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation. Amy Jose Kollannoor: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation. Kuruvilla Joseph: Investigation. Yamuna Nair: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation. Vinod T. P.: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors acknowledge Dr Mrinmoy Dey, IISc-Bangalore, for helping record CD and DLS data, Dr M. Ashraf, CIFT- Kochi for the AFM imaging, Dr Asha Thomas and Ms Anagha Jose for facilitating characterization, Mr Sai Kiran for developing the graphical abstract, and Dr Archana G.R and Dr Sreejith M for helping with references. Varsha Lisa John and Francis Joy are grateful to CHRIST (Deemed to be University) for the research fellowship. Vinod T. P. is thankful to the Centre for Research, CHRIST (Deemed to be University) for research support (Mono-graph project, MNGDSC-1806).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.03.070.

ce:section.ce:para:
